journal_entry.rationale: Recording utility expense incurred but not yet paid. Debit Utilities Expense (expense increase) to recognize the cost of utilities consumed during the period. Credit Accounts Payable (liability increase) to record the obligation to pay the utility provider. This follows the accrual basis of accounting under IFRS, recognizing expenses when incurred rather than when cash is paid. Utility bills are typically exempt from HST/GST as they are considered essential services, so no tax lines are added.

output.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recognizing the expense that has been incurred but not yet paid. The debit side records utility expense increase (1 expense line). The credit side (not classified here) would increase accounts payable/accrued liabilities.

debit_classifier.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recognizing the expense that has been incurred but not yet paid. The debit side records utility expense increase (1 expense line). The credit side (not classified here) would increase accounts payable/accrued liabilities.

output.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recognizing the expense now but not yet paying it. The credit side records the liability increase (Accounts Payable or Utilities Payable). The debit side (not classified here) would increase Utilities Expense.

credit_classifier.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recognizing the expense now but not yet paying it. The credit side records the liability increase (Accounts Payable or Utilities Payable). The debit side (not classified here) would increase Utilities Expense.

output.reason: Entry correctly records accrual of $50 utility bill. Balance verified: $50 debit = $50 credit. Utilities Expense debited (expense increase) and Accounts Payable credited (liability increase) are appropriate accounts with correct directionality. Amount matches transaction text. No tax lines needed as utility bills are typically exempt from HST/GST in Canada. Entry follows accrual basis accounting under IFRS.
